figures.gloss: The ineffable, absolute supreme being of Javanese cosmology — encompassing all existence, transcending religious dogma, and manifesting through the varied gods, spirits and human consciousness of the island. Distinct from, though later devotionally paralleled by, the Balinese Hindu 'Sang Hyang Widhi Wasa' (see balinese-hindu-acintya).
figures.facet: Highly syncretic, this concept seamlessly bridged indigenous animism, Hindu-Buddhist cosmic monism (Brahman), and Islamic Tawhid, allowing Javanese mystics to absorb incoming absolute religions without discarding their ancestral worldview.
figures.provenance: Serat Centhini (1814); Javanese oral Wayang Kulit theology.

figures.gloss: The profound, intuitive 'feeling' or 'taste' that serves as the ultimate epistemology in Kejawèn mysticism. It is the silent, inward dimension (batin) where the human soul merges directly with the divine fabric of the universe.
figures.facet: It rejects scriptural or dogmatic intellect (akal) as superficial. God is not found in texts or temples, but accessed solely through the cultivation of deep, non-conceptual internal resonance.
figures.provenance: Serat Wedhatama by Mangkunegara IV (19th century); Javanese Sufi manuscripts.

figures.gloss: The powerful, tempestuous goddess of the Indian Ocean. She claims the souls of those swept into the turbulent southern sea, especially those wearing green, and serves as the mystical spouse to the Sultans of Mataram.
figures.facet: She institutionalizes a continuous ritual marriage between terrestrial Islamic kingship and ancient, chaotic maritime animism. The Sultan's political legitimacy explicitly relies on his sexual and spiritual covenant with this oceanic goddess.
figures.provenance: Babad Tanah Jawi (Chronicle of Java, 18th century).

figures.gloss: A grotesque, farting clown servant in Javanese Wayang who is secretly Sang Hyang Ismaya, the older brother of the high god. He descends to earth to guide and serve the noble heroes of the Mahabharata.
figures.facet: He subverts the imported Hindu caste hierarchy. The lowest, ugliest peasant-servant is actually the oldest, most powerful indigenous Javanese god, proving that true divinity hides in the humble, coarse, and earthly.
figures.provenance: Kakawin Ghatotkacasraya (12th century); traditional Wayang Kulit repertoires.

figures.gloss: The leaf-shaped leather puppet used to open, transition, and close Javanese shadow puppet performances. It depicts the cosmic mountain, the tree of life, and the heavily guarded gates to the divine realm.
figures.facet: It physically manifests the philosophical concept of Manunggaling Kawula Gusti (the union of servant and lord). When planted in the banana trunk, it establishes the localized center of a temporary cosmos.
figures.provenance: Material culture of Javanese and Balinese Wayang Kulit.

figures.gloss: The deified ancestors and spiritual forces of the indigenous Marapu religion of Sumba — a Sumba-wide religion with substantial clan-to-clan and district variation, not one uniform system. They are the unseen intermediaries who reside in the high peaked roofs of traditional clan houses, demanding blood sacrifices to maintain cosmic balance.
figures.facet: They are fiercely localized and material. Spiritual transaction is not about moral salvation but strict contractual reciprocity: livestock is slaughtered so the Marapu consume the spiritual essence while humans consume the meat.
figures.provenance: Anthropological records of Sumbanese megalithic culture; Hoskins' 'The Play of Time' (1993).

figures.gloss: The 'Old Lord' and supreme creator deity of the Toraja people in Sulawesi (Aluk To Dolo). He forged the first human, animals, and plants from a divine bellows in the center of the upper world.
figures.facet: Unlike creators who speak the world into being, Puang Matua acts as a divine smith. He physically smelts and hammers the ancestors of existence out of pure gold in his celestial forge.
figures.provenance: Torajan oral epic chants (passomba tedong); Tangdilintin's 'Toraja dan Kebudayaannya' (1975).

figures.gloss: The vital life-force or soul-substance in Batak animism (Sumatra). It is an independent entity residing within the body, determining a person's fate, character, and health, but capable of wandering away or being stolen by hostile spirits.
figures.facet: The soul is not subservient to the ego. It has its own will and desires; if a person's life contradicts their Tondi's wishes, the Tondi will abandon the body, causing illness and requiring shamanic retrieval.
figures.provenance: Warneck's 'Die Religion der Batak' (1909); rituals of the Batak datu (shamans).

figures.gloss: The rice goddess, most widely venerated indigenous deity of Java, Sunda and Bali: in the origin myth her body, after death, sprouted the first rice, and every harvest since is understood as a renewal of her gift.
figures.facet: The largest single gap an Indonesian roster could carry: rice-barn shrines, planting and harvest offerings to the rice's own soul are the most common indigenous devotional acts on the island, run beneath and alongside every imported cosmology Java absorbed.
figures.provenance: Javanese and Sundanese origin-myth traditions of Dewi Sri (Nyi Pohaci in Sundanese); surveyed in standard treatments of Javanese agricultural religion.

figures.gloss: The neighborhood feast of blessed food and Arabic-Javanese prayer held at births, deaths, moves and harvests to secure slamet, a state of calm safety — binding neighbors, spirits and ancestors into a temporary equilibrium.
figures.facet: The core practiced rite of Javanese religion as Clifford Geertz described it: not a myth or a doctrine but a recurring communal meal that has to be held correctly, at the right moments of life, for the neighborhood itself to stay in balance.
figures.provenance: Clifford Geertz, The Religion of Java (1960); subsequent ethnography of Javanese ritual meals.

figures.gloss: The most beloved of the Wali Songo (Nine Saints): a reformed highwayman turned mystic who, in tradition, Islamized Java through its own arts — wayang, gamelan, the slametan — rather than against them.
figures.facet: He personifies the synthesis the tradition names but otherwise leaves unstaffed: the figure credited with making Javanese culture the vessel of Islam rather than its casualty.
figures.provenance: Javanese hagiographic tradition of the Wali Songo (babad and serat literature); standard treatments of Javanese Islamization.

figures.gloss: The prophesied Just King of the Joyoboyo prophecies, who emerges after an age of calamity to restore harmony — Java's recurrent millenarian expectation of cosmic-political renewal, repeatedly attached to living claimants from Diponegoro's war onward.
figures.facet: Java's engine of political messianism: a renewal-after-calamity structure invoked again and again across centuries of unrelated crises, each time re-anchored to a new candidate ruler.
figures.provenance: The Joyoboyo prophecy tradition (Serat Jayabaya literature) and its documented political invocations, surveyed in standard histories of Javanese millenarianism.

figures.gloss: The wavy-bladed heirloom dagger forged by an empu as a spiritually charged being: its pamor patterns are read as omens, its indwelling spirit held compatible or fatal to its owner, and it is ritually bathed and fed in the annual jamasan.
figures.facet: The paradigmatic Javanese sacred object: power (kesaktèn) and inheritance made literally into an heirloom that must be tended like a living dependent, not merely displayed.
figures.provenance: Material culture and ritual practice surrounding the Javanese keris, documented in standard studies of Indonesian sacred weaponry and empu craft tradition.

figures.gloss: The Toba Batak 'Great Beginning of Becoming': the self-existent creator seated atop the world tree, source of the three-world cosmos and of the high gods beneath him, remote from daily cult.
figures.facet: The Batak supreme creator that the roster's own tondi entry (from the same Warneck source) implies but had not named — completing the source's own cosmology rather than adding an unrelated figure.
figures.provenance: Johannes Warneck, Die Religion der Batak (1909); standard ethnography of Toba Batak cosmology.

figures.gloss: The ruling high god of the Javanese wayang pantheon, seated atop Mount Mahameru in Kahyangan Jonggring Salaka: son of Sang Hyang Tunggal and, in wayang genealogy, elder god-brother of Semar/Ismaya. It is Batara Guru, not Tunggal, whom Hindu-Buddhist Java identified with Shiva.
figures.facet: The roster's Shiva-identification edge was misattached to the transcendent, undifferentiated Tunggal; Batara Guru is the actual ruling, personal, iconographically Shiva-styled god of the wayang court, and belongs in the pantheon Tunggal only underlies.
figures.provenance: Wayang Kulit genealogical tradition (Sang Hyang Tunggal's sons: Batara Guru and Ismaya/Semar); standard treatments of Javanese Hindu-Buddhist syncretism.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The transcendent, abstract, and celestial absolute unity contrasted against the chaotic, devouring, specific feminine power of the ocean.
Intra-tradition: The hidden earthly god (Semar/Ismaya) is the older, humbler brother of the transcendent high god, acting as his earthly shadow.
Intra-tradition: The Gunungan visually maps the inner, silent spiritual ascent of Rasa toward the ultimate union with the divine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme deity was heavily identified with Shiva (Batara Guru) during the Hindu-Buddhist period of Java.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Syncretized with the ultimate Hindu cosmic principle through Majapahit court theology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explicitly merged with the Islamic concept of Tawhid by the Wali Songo (Nine Saints) to Islamize the Javanese interior.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A powerful, tempestuous goddess of the deep ocean who commands souls and controls maritime bounty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The personification of the sea as a maternal but dangerous power requiring constant appeasement.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A trickster figure who mediates between the human and divine, possessing supreme spiritual authority despite a deceptive exterior.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The grotesque, dog-like or clownish twin of the high god who descends to aid humanity and navigate the lower realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The 'failed' or demoted primal being whose disruption forms the necessary foundation for human culture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A cosmic tree that centers the universe and acts as the gate between the earthly and divine realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Javanese shadow puppet physically represents and descends directly from Hindu-Buddhist cosmology of Mount Meru.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A creator deity who operates not as a speaker of words, but as a master craftsman forging life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A divine smith whose forge is the center of mythical creation and sustenance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A semi-autonomous soul entity whose detachment or wandering from the physical body is the primary cause of illness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unnameable, intuitive inner current that serves as both the fabric of the universe and the method to understand it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Inner, intuitive, mystical wisdom that bypasses the written law and intellect of the orthodox creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deified ancestors demanding strict ritual and blood appeasement to maintain social order and protect the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The powerful dead who remain active agents in their descendants' affairs, anchoring the community's spiritual architecture.
Two Southeast Asian rice goddesses whose death, burial, or bodily gift is the mythic origin of the rice plant itself — independent regional parallels, not claimed descent.

Intra-tradition: both are technologies for opening a temporary sacred equilibrium — the slametan's shared meal binding neighbors, spirits and ancestors; the gunungan's planting opening the wayang's temporary cosmos.
Tradition credits Kalijaga, chief of the Wali Songo, with personally effecting the synthesis of Tunggal and Tawhid that the existing Tunggal-Allah seam in this file documents doctrinally.
Intra-tradition: the saint credited with reconciling indigenous Tunggal cosmology and Islamic revelation without erasing either.
A prophesied just ruler ending an age of calamity and restoring order — an independent messianic-renewal structure; no contact claimed.
Two remote, self-existent supreme creators invoked rarely in daily cult, standing prior to or atop the working cosmos — an Austronesian-family structural echo, not documented contact.
Intra-tradition: both documented from the same Batak ethnographic source (Warneck) — the supreme creator and the soul-substance he ultimately underlies in Toba Batak cosmology.

(corrected attachment): the Hindu-Buddhist Shiva identification documented in Javanese wayang genealogy belongs to Batara Guru, ruler of the gods and Tunggal's son — see flagged note on the existing Tunggal-Shiva edge.
Intra-tradition: father and son in wayang genealogy — Batara Guru is Tunggal's ruling son, ruler of the pantheon.
Intra-tradition: brothers in wayang genealogy — Batara Guru the exalted ruling brother, Semar/Ismaya the humble elder brother who descends to serve mortals.

dossier.essence: Kejawen (also kebatinan or Javanism) is the mystical-ethical worldview of many Javanese of Indonesia — a syncretic 'inner' spirituality fusing indigenous animism, Hindu-Buddhist metaphysics, and Islamic (especially Sufi) elements, associated with the nominally Muslim abangan Javanese. In this atlas the record also gathers other Indonesian indigenous religions as neighboring shards: Marapu of Sumba, Aluk To Dolo of the Toraja in Sulawesi, and Batak belief in Sumatra.
dossier.cosmos: Kejawen tends toward monism — one ineffable source (Sang Hyang Widhi Wasa / Tunggal) manifest through all gods, spirits, and selves — the mystic's goal being manunggaling kawula Gusti, the union of servant and Lord. Reality is doubled into a visible outer (lair) and a felt inner (batin), the latter the true seat of the sacred; potent local powers such as the ocean-goddess Nyai Roro Kidul remain woven into Javanese kingship. The bundled indigenous systems are more concretely ancestral: the Toraja route the dead to Puya, the land of souls, through elaborate funerals, and the Sumbanese feed the marapu ancestors with sacrificed buffalo and pigs.
dossier.practice: Kejawen is cultivated through meditation and asceticism (semadi, tapa, ritual fasting), the slametan communal ritual meal, deference to a guru, and wayang kulit shadow-theatre received as moral and cosmological teaching rather than mere entertainment. The gathered indigenous traditions center on ancestor-feasting, megalithic tomb-building, and — among the Toraja — monumental multi-day funerary rites with buffalo sacrifice.
dossier.canon: No single scripture: Kejawen draws on Javanese court literature — the Serat Centhini (1814) and the Serat Wedhatama of Mangkunegara IV — and the oral wayang repertoire, while Marapu, Aluk To Dolo, and Batak belief are transmitted orally.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds interiority — the conviction that the divine is reached through cultivated inner feeling (rasa) rather than through doctrine, scripture, or law.
sources: Niels Mulder, 'Mysticism in Java: Ideology in Indonesia' (1998)
Andrew Beatty, 'Varieties of Javanese Religion: An Anthropological Account' (1999)
Janet Hoskins, 'The Play of Time: Kodi Perspectives on Calendars, History, and Exchange' (1993)
